column1:
Carotid body tumors are more common in:

A: men and people with Buerger disease
B: men and people with Ehler-Danlos syndrome
C: women and people living at sea level
D: women and people living at high altitudes
column2:
Answer: D. women and people living at high altitudes

Feedback:
CBT is more common in females and those living at higher altitudes. Higher elevations mean less oxygen in the air. Chronic hypoxia leads to an overactive and enlarged CBT.

column1:
Which of the following carries an increased risk of pulmonary embolism?

A: portal vein thrombosis
B: renal vein thrombosis
C: thrombus accumulation in an aortic aneurysm
D: common femoral artery thrombosis
column2:
Answer: B. renal vein thrombosis

Feedback:
A clot in the systemic venous system can break loose and travel to the lungs. The renal veins drain into the IVC. The portal system does not directly communicate with the systemic veins, so a clot in the portal vein cannot lead to pulmonary embolism. An embolus in the arterial system will only travel until it becomes lodged in a smaller distal artery and cause ischemia.

column1:
Which statement is true regarding the image?

A: The patient should be evaluated for neointimal hyperplasia.
B: There is calcification of the vessels in the vasa vasorum in the arterial wall and the patient should have balloon angioplasty.
C: This patient should have an angiogram to confirm the suspected diagnosis of fibromuscular dysplasia.
D: There is calcification of the vessels in the vasa vasorum in the arterial wall and the patient should have a TCD exam to evaluate the extent of the disease.
column2:
Answer: A. The patient should be evaluated for neointimal hyperplasia.

Feedback:
The image shows a stent in the carotid artery. Neointimal hyperplasia refers to the overgrowth of normal tissue in response to the endarterectomy and stent/graft placement. This is a common cause of re-stenosis of a vessel that previously underwent interventional treatment.

column1:
What is the most likely cause of the findings on the image?

A: bilateral iliac stenosis
B: bilateral popliteal entrapment
C: embolism
D: bilateral peroneal artery occlusion
column2:
Answer: C. embolism

Feedback:
Normal ABIs with abnormal digit tracings indicate obstruction of the digital arteries by embolisms. The patient should be evaluated for AAA for a source for the emboli. Popliteal entrapment requires maneuvers during the exams and the calf arteries and digital arteries would be affected. Bilateral iliac stenosis would cause abnormal waveforms in all areas in both legs.

column1:
How can the gastrocnemius veins and the posterior tibial veins be differentiated on an ultrasound exam?

A: Document the direction of flow relative to the foot
B: Document the direction of flow relative to the knee
C: Follow them distally to locate their point of origination
D: Document the presence of a corresponding artery
column2:
Answer: C. Follow them distally to locate their point of origination

Feedback:
Gastrocnemius veins originate deep within the calf muscle and extend to the popliteal fossa to join the popliteal vein. The posterior tibial veins originate at the ankle and extend to join the tibioperoneal trunk near the popliteal fossa. If you are progressing down the leg for a venous evaluation, when you reach the popliteal fossa and begin noting the calf vessels, pick a set to follow. If they dive deep into the calf = gastrocnemius veins, if they extend to the ankle = PTVs. Both sets of veins have a corresponding artery and flow moving toward the heart. These characteristics would not differentiate them from each other.

column1:
An acute occlusion occurs in the common iliac artery on the left side. Which of the following vessels will also have absent flow?

A: external iliac artery only
B: common femoral artery and saphenous artery
C: internal iliac artery and gonadal artery
D: common femoral, external iliac and internal iliac artery
column2:
Answer: D. common femoral, external iliac and internal iliac artery

Feedback:
The external iliac and internal iliac arteries are branches of the common iliac artery. The external iliac artery becomes the common femoral artery once it crosses beneath the inguinal artery.

column1:
A patient presents with a 2-year history of a brachiocephalic hemodialysis fistula. She currently complains that her hand goes numb and turns white when she exercises. You are performing an upper extremity arterial exam and the attached images are from her exam. What will you include in your report?

A: The exam is normal and does not explain the patient's symptoms.
B: Steal syndrome is present.
C: There is an obstruction in the palmar arch.
D: The proximal radial artery is occluded
column2:
Answer: B. Steal syndrome is present.

Feedback:
Steal syndrome can occur with a hemodialysis graft. Flow moves down the arm through the ulnar artery, through the palmar arch to the radial artery. Blood flow takes the path of least resistance. Because the pressure in the outflow vein is so low, flow reverses in the radial artery as the blood is sucked up the arm and into the cephalic vein. When the patient exercises, the demand for blood increases in the hand but the ulnar artery cannot supply enough by itself = symptoms.

column1:
What should be done to adjust and improve the color displayed on the attached image?

A: Increase velocity scale
B: Decrease color baseline
C: Steer color box
D: Increase PRF
column2:
Answer: C. Steer color box

Feedback:
The goal for color Doppler processing is to demonstrate blood that is moving in a direction parallel to the US beam. Vessels displayed in longitudinal plane are more perpendicular to the beam. By angling the probe and the color box you cause the flow to be more parallel to the beam. Color Doppler display improves when the flow is more parallel in relationship to the beam.

column1:
The seagull sign refers to which of the following vessels?

A: bilateral renal origins
B: three hepatic veins
C: common femoral bifurcation
D: celiac axis
column2:
Answer: D. celiac axis

Feedback:
The celiac axis and the two branches visible on US form the seagull sign. The CHA and Splenic artery form the wings of the bird.

column1:
Which of the following waveforms is most consistent with flow related to a pseudoaneurysm in the groin area?

A: D
B: C
C: A
D: B
column2:
Answer: C. A

Feedback:
Flow in the neck and body of a pseudoaneurysm is described as "to-and-fro". Waveform A is from a pseudoaneurysm in the groin. Flow moves in and out of the blind ended pocket in the tissues. Waveform B is a normal triphasic waveform from a lower extremity. Waveform C is a normal waveform from the renal parenchyma. Waveform D is taken from a vertebral artery and demonstrates early systolic deceleration. It is associated with proximal stenosis and considered a pre-steal phenomenon.

column1:
Which of the following would cause flow in the left ECA to reverse direction during systole?

A: left CCA occlusion
B: left vertebral artery occlusion
C: right CCA occlusion
D: left proximal ICA occlusion
column2:
Answer: A. left CCA occlusion

Feedback:
Ipsilateral CCA occlusion can cause ECA flow reversal so that it can supply the ICA with flow. Collateral flow from the contralateral cerebral system will back fill the left ECA. Reversed flow in the ECA will fill the left ICA with blood because the CCA is occluded.

column1:
Which of the following statements regarding venous perforators is correct?

A: They each contain a single valve.
B: Thrombosis rarely occurs within the perforators and it cannot to be easily demonstrated on ultrasound evaluation.
C: Perforator flow is normally unidirectional, but becomes bidirectional with exercise.
D: Flow in the perforators normally moves from the deep to the superficial system.
column2:
Answer: A. They each contain a single valve.

Feedback:
Venous valves are designed to allow unidirectional flow. If the valves are damaged or the vein is dilated to prevent proper valve movement, flow reversal may occur. Flow in the perforators normally moves from the superficial to the deep system. They each contain a single valve. Thrombosis can occur within the perforators, which can lead to the formation of varicosities.

column1:
At what level in the neck does the common carotid artery normally bifurcate?

A: At the upper level of the thyrocervical trunk
B: At the upper border of the thyroid cartilage
C: At the lower border of the larynx
D: At the angle of the mandible
column2:
Answer: B. At the upper border of the thyroid cartilage

Feedback:
The common carotid artery normally bifurcates at the level of the upper border of the thyroid cartilage.

column1:
Pulse volume recording:

A: will demonstrate waveforms that are nearly identical to the analog Doppler waveforms in a normal patient
B: is used to assess arterial insufficiency and DVT
C: is commonly performed with a segmental pressure exam
D: readings should be taken beginning with the distal cuff and moving proximally
column2:
Answer: C. is commonly performed with a segmental pressure exam

Feedback:
Pulse volume recording is used to assess arterial disease. PVR exams are usually performed with segmental pressure exams. Readings are taken first in the proximal extremity and then moving distally to the next cuff. The normal waveform will be monophasic with rapid upstroke, sharp systolic peak, prolonged downstroke and prominent reflection (dicrotic notch). Normal Doppler tracings would be triphasic.

column1:
A patient presents for a pre-procedure scan for placement of a central venous catheter in the internal jugular vein. In order to avoid potential hematoma or AV fistula formation, the relationship of the vein to the _________________________ must be documented on today's exam.

A: external jugular vein
B: common carotid artery
C: sternocleidomastoid muscle
D: internal carotid artery
column2:
Answer: B. common carotid artery

Feedback:
A patient presents for a pre-procedure scan for placement of a central venous catheter in the internal jugular vein. In order to avoid potential hematoma or AV fistula formation, the relationship of the vein to the common carotid artery must be documented on today's exam. If the CCA is punctured, serious complications can occur.

column1:
What is the purpose of performing periorbital Doppler?

A: To evaluate the ophthalmic artery using Doppler
B: Detect flow reversal in the frontal artery signifying ICA disease
C: Detect severe distal ECA disease
D: Evaluate the integrity of the retina
column2:
Answer: B. Detect flow reversal in the frontal artery signifying ICA disease

Feedback:
Periorbital Doppler is used to assess flow direction in the frontal artery. If flow is moving away from the transducer, ipsilateral ICA disease is present. The prefix peri- indicates the area surrounding the eye. TRANSorbital Doppler is performed to evaluate the ophthalmic artery.

column1:
When performing a lower extremity venous Doppler for insufficiency,

A: augmentation pressure is applied quickly, held for at least 15 seconds, and released quickly
B: the GSV should be evaluated with patient bearing weight on the leg
C: a minimum of 30 seconds between augmentation maneuvers is recommended
D: the intensity of the Doppler signal indicates the severity of the reflux
column2:
Answer: C. a minimum of 30 seconds between augmentation maneuvers is recommended

Feedback:
When performing a lower extremity venous Doppler for insufficiency, a minimum of 30 seconds between augmentation maneuvers is recommended. The veins need sufficient time to refill before augmentation is repeated.

column1:
A patient presents for a follow up exam on a lower extremity femoral-popliteal in-situ vein graft that was placed in the right leg 2 weeks ago. He is experiencing pain, pallor and paresthesia in the right foot. Which of the following is not an expected finding on the exam?

A: anastomosis narrowed by an aberrant suture
B: thrombosis around a valve remnant
C: graft kinking
D: extension of atherosclerotic plaque into the graft
column2:
Answer: D. extension of atherosclerotic plaque into the graft

Feedback:
Atherosclerosis is a chronic disease and would not progress into the area of the graft in such a short time period. The other choices listed are all potential acute complications following graft placement.

column1:
The carotid siphon:

A: causes an area of decreased resistance to flow in the distal ICA
B: usually produces a waveform with a clear spectral window
C: supplies blood to the ophthalmic artery
D: is an area of flow separation due to the increase in vessel diameter at the bulb
column2:
Answer: C. supplies blood to the ophthalmic artery

Feedback:
ICA Siphon - distal ICA segment that courses anteriorly then medially, then posteriorly forming an "S". The tortuous path leads to reduced flow, increased resistance, and causes turbulence. There will be no spectral window visible due to the tortuous course of the vessel. The siphon has increased risk for flow reducing lesions, such as atheroma and thrombus. The ophthalmic artery branches from the carotid siphon.

column1:
Which of the following describes an important reason to verify the US examination that is ordered is the exam that should really be performed?

A: When the exam is ordered after hours and no reading physician is available
B: When the suspected diagnosis is not able to be evaluated by the exam that is ordered
C: Sonographers should not question the orders written by physicians and perform the exams as ordered.
D: When the suspected abnormality is better visualized by another modality
column2:
Answer: B. When the suspected diagnosis is not able to be evaluated by the exam that is ordered

Feedback:
When the suspected diagnosis is not able to be evaluated by the exam that is ordered, this is an important reason to verify the US examination that is ordered is the exam that should really be performed.

column1:
Which of the following is a characteristic of a normal lower extremity arterial PVR waveform?

A: slow upslope
B: slow downslope
C: low amplitude
D: prominent dicrotic notch
column2:
Answer: D. prominent dicrotic notch

Feedback:
An abnormal PVR waveform will demonstrate a loss of the dicrotic notch, slow upslope, slow downslope and little difference between systolic and diastolic blood volumes.

column1:
A patient presents with bilateral distention of the internal jugular veins and arm swelling. The lower extremities are normal on physical examination. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: Congestive heart failure
B: Subclavian steal syndrome
C: Interrupted IVC
D: SVC Syndrome
column2:
Answer: D. SVC Syndrome

Feedback:
SVC syndrome refers to the progressive obstruction of the SVC which causes venous dilatation of the upper extremities and neck. Doppler evaluation of the bilateral internal jugular veins will demonstrate continuous flow patterns. Respiratory phasicity and cardiac pulsatility will be lost in the bilateral IJVs due to the SVC obstruction.

column1:
A patient presents with episodes of left eye amaurosis fugax and right sided paresthesia. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: 80% LT ECA stenosis
B: RT ICA occlusion
C: LT CCA stenosis proximal to current point of evaluation
D: LT ICA occlusion
column2:
Answer: D. LT ICA occlusion

Feedback:
The Left CCA waveform demonstrates very high resistance flow with flow reversal in diastole. This is indicative of a LT ICA occlusion distally. The ipsilateral visual disturbance and contralateral limb paresthesia also support this diagnosis.

column1:
The Adson maneuver is helpful in the diagnosis of what vascular disorder?

A: Venous insufficiency
B: Collateral formation with carotid occlusion
C: Thoracic outlet syndrome
D: Raynaud syndrome
column2:
Answer: C. Thoracic outlet syndrome

Feedback:
The Adson maneuver is a patient position used to evaluate flow changes with thoracic outlet syndrome. Palpate the radial pulse on the affected side with the elbow fully extended. Have the patient rotate their head to the side being tested and extend the neck. Then abduct, extend, and laterally rotate the shoulder. From this position, have the patient take a deep breath and hold. Assess the pulse response. A positive test is a decrease in pulse vigor from the starting position to the final position.

column1:
Venous flow proximal to an AV fistula becomes ________________ due to the inflow of arterial flow distally

A: phasic
B: high resistance
C: continuous
D: pulsatile
column2:
Answer: D. pulsatile

Feedback:
Venous flow proximal to an AV fistula becomes pulsatile due to the inflow of arterial flow distally. The image below demonstrates the venous outflow of a fistula (proximal to the fistula). The flow in the cephalic vein is turbulent and pulsatile.

column1: Use your mouse to position the cursor over the anterior accessory saphenous vein and click to set the cursor.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The anterior accessory saphenous vein travels anterior to the femoral artery to join the great saphenous vein near its junction with the common femoral vein at the groin. When visualized on an exam in a patient with symptoms of venous insufficiency, it should be evaluated with color and PW Doppler for signs of reflux.

column1:
If the distal femoral artery demonstrates a peak velocity of 3.2m/s, the waveform in the posterior tibial artery will most likely be _____________.

A: monophasic
B: absent
C: triphasic
D: biphasic
column2:
Answer: A. monophasic

Feedback:
Normal flow in the PTA would be triphasic. A 3.2m/s PSV in the femoral artery indicates a hemodynamically significant stenosis which would cause decreased resistance distally. The popliteal and calf arterial waveforms will most likely be monophasic.

column1:
Which of the following statements regarding a Duplex exam of the upper extremity veins is correct?

A: The cephalic vein is easily compressed with light probe pressure, which can be an exam limitation.
B: The subclavian and innominate veins normally demonstrate continuous flow.
C: Inspiration will cause a decrease in flow within the larger upper extremity veins.
D: The internal jugular vein cannot be evaluated by ultrasound due to its course through bony structures of the neck.
column2:
Answer: A. The cephalic vein is easily compressed with light probe pressure, which can be an exam limitation.

Feedback:
The cephalic vein is easily compressed with light probe pressure, which can be an exam limitation. The basilic vein is also easily compressible due to its location. Proper compression of the subclavian vein is normally inhibited by the clavicle. The subclavian and innominate veins usually demonstrate pulsatile, phasic flow due to their proximity to the heart/lungs. The IJV should be evaluated on all upper extremity venous duplex exams. It courses through the soft tissue/muscle of the neck, adjacent to the carotid artery. Inspiration will cause a decrease in flow within the larger upper extremity veins.

column1:
The lower extremity segmental pressure exam is most commonly performed with a/an ___________________ to obtain the Doppler signal for the pressure measurements at the ankle.

A: 2-3MHz CW Doppler probe
B: 8-10Mhz vector array
C: 5-7MHz linear array
D: 8-10MHz CW Doppler probe
column2:
Answer: D. 8-10MHz CW Doppler probe

Feedback:
The dedicated CW probe is used to obtain the Doppler signal at the ankle while pressure measurements are obtained in the leg.

column1:
Which of the following cardiac abnormalities will have no effect on venous flow characteristics in the lower extremities?

A: severe tricuspid stenosis
B: premature ventricular contractions
C: significant tricuspid regurgitation
D: congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: B. premature ventricular contractions

Feedback:
Premature ventricular contractions are a type of cardiac arrhythmia. A PVC will cause smaller ectopic contractions before a larger compensatory contraction. This abnormality alone will not affect venous flow characteristics in the extremities. The other choices lead to a backlog of flow in the IVC. When the IVC/iliac veins are dilated and engorged with blood, the cardiac pulsatility is transmitted further away from the heart due to the ripple effect.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes sclerotherapy procedures?

A: Physicians may inject saline into the deep veins of the extremity in order to increase viscosity and promote the formation of thrombus, which in turn reduces varicosity size.
B: Physicians may inject saline into the superficial veins of the extremity in order to cause the vessel to contract and cause fibrosis, which in turn reduces varicosity size.
C: Physicians may inject saline into the superficial veins of the extremity in order to cause increased viscosity and promote the formation of thrombus, which in turn reduces varicosity size.
D: Physicians may inject saline into the superficial veins of the extremity in order to improve circulation by thinning the blood, which in turn reduces varicosity size.
column2:
Answer: B. Physicians may inject saline into the superficial veins of the extremity in order to cause the vessel to contract and cause fibrosis, which in turn reduces varicosity size.

Feedback:
Physicians may inject saline into the superficial veins of the extremity in order to cause the vessel to contract and cause fibrosis, which in turn reduces varicosity size.

column1:
Which of the following describes the proper way to measure an abdominal aortic aneurysm?

A: outer wall to inner wall
B: inner wall to inner wall
C: outer wall to outer wall
D: inner wall to outer wall
column2:
Answer: C. outer wall to outer wall

Feedback:
The aorta should be measured from outer wall to outer wall. The AP dimension is considered the most reliable dimension for assessing a possible AAA.

column1:
What effect will the findings on the image have on the Doppler tracings obtained in the iliac veins?

A: Both iliac veins will demonstrate continuous flow
B: Both iliac veins will demonstrate pulsatile flow
C: Both iliac veins will demonstrate phasic flow
D: Both iliac veins will demonstrate normal flow patterns due to the extensive venous network in the abdomen that allows for multiple collateral pathways
column2:
Answer: A. Both iliac veins will demonstrate continuous flow

Feedback:
Renal carcinoma has been shown to infiltrate the renal vein and IVC causing mass and thrombus formation within. It is the most common type of tumor with IVC/renal vein invasion. Obstruction of the IVC will cause flow in the iliac veins to become continuous. Continuous flow in an extremity vein typically indicates a proximal obstruction is present.

column1:
When considering the effectiveness of the carotid Doppler exam at your lab, the negative predictive value is 88%, the positive predictive value is 95%, the sensitivity is 85% and the specificity is 90%. Using those parameters, which of the following could be the value for the accuracy of the testing technique?

A: 84%
B: 89%
C: 92%
D: 96%
column2:
Answer: B. 89%

Feedback:
The accuracy of a technique falls between the NPV and PPV AND between the sensitivity and specificity. 89% is higher than 88%NPV and lower than 95%PPV; 89% is also higher than 85% sensitivity and lower than 90% specificity.

column1:
Which of the following acronyms refers to the standard image file storage format used in medical facilities in the United States?

A: MPEG
B: JPEG
C: AVI
D: DICOM
column2:
Answer: D. DICOM

Feedback:
DICOM - Digital Imaging & COmmunications in Medicine provides a standard for handling, storing, and transmitting information in medical imaging.

column1:
Which of the following is the best patient position for performing a transcranial Doppler exam to obtain a waveform from the basilar artery?

A: seated on a stool, facing the Sonographer and the machine
B: left lateral decubitus with left hand placed under the head for support
C: seated on a stool, with their back to the Sonographer and the machine
D: supine with chin in neutral position
column2:
Answer: B. left lateral decubitus with left hand placed under the head for support

Feedback:
The patient needs to be able to hold their head steady for the exam. Lying down with the head supported will provide the best position for the exam. Placing the hand under the head will also help to raise it from the stretcher to maintain a straight neck.

column1:
When performing post-occlusive reactive hyperemia, pressure measurements in the lower extremities are obtained:

A: immediately after cuff release and every 30 seconds until pressures return to baseline levels
B: 1 minute after cuff release and every 30 seconds until pressures return to baseline levels
C: 1 minute after cuff release and every 2 minutes until pressures return to baseline levels
D: immediately after cuff release and every 2 minutes until pressures return to baseline levels
column2:
Answer: A. immediately after cuff release and every 30 seconds until pressures return to baseline levels

Feedback:
When performing post-occlusive reactive hyperemia of the lower extremities, pressure measurements are obtained immediately after cuff release and every 30 seconds until pressures return to baseline levels. Recovery time is shorter for reactive hyperemia than treadmill testing.

column1: Using the 3rd waveform, place the first cursor used to begin the resistive index measurement. Use your mouse to position the cursor and click to set your answer.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
Normal renal artery waveforms demonstrate an early systolic peak. When measuring the AT, the first caliper is placed at the start of systole and the second caliper is placed at the top of the early systolic peak. When measuring the resistive index, the first caliper is placed at the true peak systolic velocity and the second caliper is placed at the peak end diastolic velocity.

column1:
Which of the following is considered a risk factor for peripheral arterial disease?

A: Excessive, repetitive standing
B: Pregnancy
C: History of deep vein thrombosis
D: Diabetes
column2:
Answer: D. Diabetes

Feedback:
Diabetes will expedite hardening of the arterial walls, especially in smaller arteries. The calf and digital arteries are first affected by medial calcification. Pregnancy and excessive standing can lead to an increase in venous blood volume and stasis but has no effect on the formation of arterial disease. A history of DVT does not affect the risk of atherosclerotic disease.

column1:
When providing a preliminary report for a significant ICA stenosis, what should be included?

A: peak systolic and end diastolic velocities in the common and internal carotid arteries
B: end systolic velocities in the common and internal carotid arteries
C: hemispheric index for both carotids
D: angles of insonation used to obtain Doppler signals
column2:
Answer: A. peak systolic and end diastolic velocities in the common and internal carotid arteries

Feedback:
The peak systolic AND end diastolic velocities should be included in the report. The hemispheric index is used in TCD evaluation to compare MCA and ICA velocities. The Doppler angles do not need to be included in the preliminary report provided by the technologist but should be included in the final report. Most ultrasound systems transfer this information on each image, along with in chart form at the end of the exam.

column1:
Which of the following describes the correct protocol for evaluating suspected popliteal entrapment?

A: Use Doppler to evaluate the flow in the popliteal artery at rest and with plantar flexion
B: Evaluate the toe pressures at rest and during plantar extension
C: Use Doppler to evaluate the flow in the popliteal vein at rest and with plantar flexion
D: Use Doppler to evaluate the flow in the distal femoral artery at rest and with plantar extension
column2:
Answer: A. Use Doppler to evaluate the flow in the popliteal artery at rest and with plantar flexion

Feedback:
Popliteal entrapment involves the intermittent compression of the popliteal artery with flexing of the calf muscle. Arterial inflow is reduced causing pain in the calf muscle and foot due to ischemia. The velocity of the flow in the vessel is important to evaluate the degree of stenosis that is caused by movement. PPG is the preferred method of evaluation, but Doppler evaluation can also be used.

column1:
Longitudinal images are presented of the anterior abdomen just superior to the umbilicus. What is the most common cause of the abnormality seen on the images?

A: portal HTN
B: polycythemia vera
C: malignant HTN
D: pulmonary HTN
column2:
Answer: A. portal HTN

Feedback:
A patent umbilical vein is identified on the images. Severe portal HTN will cause the umbilical vein to recanalize, allowing retrograde filling to occur within the vessel.

column1:
Which transcranial window is most commonly used to evaluate the carotid siphon and the ophthalmic artery?

A: temporal
B: submandibular
C: orbital
D: occipital
column2:
Answer: C. orbital

Feedback:
The orbital or ophthalmic window is used to evaluate the carotid siphon and the ophthalmic artery. If the siphon cannot be evaluated on this view, the supplemental submandibular approach may be used.

column1:
Using the transtemporal window, the normal middle cerebral artery ______________________.

A: will demonstrate flow away from the probe
B: will demonstrate biphasic flow
C: will demonstrate flow toward the probe
D: will demonstrate a clear spectral window
column2:
Answer: C. will demonstrate flow toward the probe

Feedback:
Using the transtemporal window, the normal MCA will demonstrate monophasic flow toward the probe. Because the intracranial vessels are so small, no spectral window is demonstrated on the waveform.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid ultrasound due to the presence of bilateral bruits in the proximal neck. What should be the next step in evaluating this patient?

A: Evaluate the cardiac history section of the patient chart to look for a history of aortic valve stenosis.
B: Evaluate the vascular history section of the patient chart to look for a history of a subclavian steal
C: Evaluate the cardiac history section of the patient chart to look for a history of CHF.
D: Evaluate the medical history section of the patient chart to look for a history of polycythemia vera
column2:
Answer: A. Evaluate the cardiac history section of the patient chart to look for a history of aortic valve stenosis.

Feedback:
Bilateral carotid bruits and changes in both carotid flow patterns can be related to aortic stenosis. If the aortic valve is stenotic causing increased flow velocity and turbulence, flow in both carotids will reflect this change and may demonstrate bilateral bruits. CHF would lead to decreased flow through both carotids but a bruit would most likely NOT be identified due to slow moving flow. Polycythemia vera causes the blood to thicken = slower velocities = reduced risk of bilateral bruit. A subclavian steal involves the rerouting of blood and would not be associated with a bilateral bruit.

column1:
Transcranial Doppler most commonly uses which Doppler measurement to evaluate flow in the cerebral vessels?

A: peak systolic velocity
B: time average maximum velocity
C: end diastolic velocity
D: resistive index
column2:
Answer: B. time average maximum velocity

Feedback:
Time average maximum velocity or mean flow velocity is used to assess flow in the cerebral arteries.

column1:
The superficial venous system of the lower extremities contains vessels that are located

A: on the lateral aspect of each leg
B: within the connective tissue between the muscle tissue
C: superficial to the deep muscular fascia
D: adjacent to an artery with the same name
column2:
Answer: C. superficial to the deep muscular fascia

Feedback:
The saphenous veins and their tributaries course within the subcutaneous tissue, between the fascial layers.

column1:
Which of the following principles produces an equation that relates pressure gradient to flow and resistance within the circulatory system?

A: Poiseuille's Law
B: Bernoulli Principle
C: Doppler Principle
D: Raynaud's Phenomenon
column2:
Answer: A. Poiseuille's Law

Feedback:
Poiseuille's Law states the pressure gradient in a vessel is equal to the flow volume multiplied by the resistance. Bernoulli's equation uses flow velocity to determine the pressure gradient at a specific location within the circulatory system.

column1:
Which of the following describes the appearance of a waveform distal to a critical carotid stenosis?

A: increased diastolic flow
B: decreased diastolic flow
C: increased systolic flow
D: large spectral window
column2:
Answer: B. decreased diastolic flow

Feedback:
The waveform of the flow distal to a critical ICA stenosis will be blunted, low systolic and diastolic peaks and turbulent flow with loss of the spectral window.

column1:
Which of the following normally demonstrates a higher resistance Doppler flow profile?

A: external carotid artery
B: post-prandial superior mesenteric artery
C: renal artery
D: hepatic artery
column2:
Answer: A. external carotid artery

Feedback:
"Organ flow" will typically be low resistance and constant flow is needed for the organs to function. The post prandial SMA will be lower resistance with increased diastolic flow due to increased digestive system function. The renal and hepatic arteries will also have low resistance flow with increased diastolic flow because they constantly function. "Extremity flow" is typically high resistance as muscles/tissue need much less inflow, especially at rest. The external carotid artery and its branches feed mainly muscles and tissue, therefore the flow demonstrates a higher resistance waveform.

column1:
Which of the following is the most likely cause for the waveform displayed?

A: stenosis of the internal iliac artery
B: peripheral arterial disease
C: vasoconstriction in the pedal arteries
D: venous insufficiency
column2:
Answer: B. peripheral arterial disease

Feedback:
The image demonstrates an abnormal monophasic flow pattern in the PTA. Peripheral arterial disease can cause this abnormal flow pattern. Venous insufficiency does not affect the arterial inflow. Vasoconstriction of the pedal arteries would increase the resistance to flow in the calf arteries. Stenosis of the internal iliac arteries would cause ischemia to the pelvic organs, not the extremity.

column1:
Which of the following factors are related to potential hemodynamic changes within a vessel?

A: vessel length and blood viscosity, but not vessel radius
B: blood viscosity and vessel radius, but not vessel length
C: vessel length and vessel radius, but not blood viscosity
D: vessel length, vessel radius and blood viscosity
column2:
Answer: D. vessel length, vessel radius and blood viscosity

Feedback:
Changes in vessel length, vessel radius and blood viscosity are related to potential hemodynamic changes within a vessel. Know Poiseuille's Law!

column1:
A patient presents for a stat lower extremity arterial Doppler evaluation following the recent placement of a fem-tib graft in the right leg. The patient complains of significant pain and weakness in the calf muscle with areas of numbness on the anterior aspect of the lower leg. What is the most likely finding on the exam?

A: graft stenosis
B: anterior compartment syndrome
C: DVT
D: phlegmasia alba dolens
column2:
Answer: B. anterior compartment syndrome

Feedback:
Reperfusion of the leg via bypass graft can lead to anterior compartment syndrome. Symptoms include paresthesia, pain in the calf muscle with foot dorsiflexion, drop foot and calf muscle weakness.

column1:
Which of the following is a characteristic of an ICA waveform with 50-69% stenosis?

A: decreased diastolic flow with EDV less than 20cm/s
B: increased peak systolic and end diastolic velocity
C: diastolic flow reversal
D: clear spectral window
column2:
Answer: B. increased peak systolic and end diastolic velocity

Feedback:
Stenosis in the ICA leads to increased systolic and diastolic flow velocities. Spectral broadening occurs with stenosis. Diastolic flow reversal in the ICA is identified with an occlusion distal to the Doppler sample.

column1:
Which blood vessel courses anterior to the aorta and posterior to the SMA?

A: left renal vein
B: right renal vein
C: inferior mesenteric vein
D: superior mesenteric vein
column2:
Answer: A. left renal vein

Feedback:
The left renal vein courses from the left kidney across the abdomen between the aorta and SMA to reach the IVC.

column1:
If an acute left ICA occlusion is present, which of the following statements is true?

A: The right MCA flow will be decreased or absent with flow reversal in the right internal carotid siphon.
B: The right MCA will demonstrate decreased or absent flow.
C: The left ophthalmic artery will demonstrate decreased or absent flow
D: The right ophthalmic artery will demonstrate decreased or absent flow
column2:
Answer: C. The left ophthalmic artery will demonstrate decreased or absent flow

Feedback:
The ICA supplies the ipsilateral MCA and ophthalmic artery with blood. If the ICA is occluded, the flow in these vessels will be absent unless collateral vessels are present which would lead to minimal flow in the vessels. Collaterals would not be present with an acute occlusion.

column1:
You are performing a carotid Doppler exam due to the recent onset of right arm and face paresthesia. There is little plaque present in either carotid system. The right side has normal Doppler flow velocities and waveforms. The attached Doppler images are taken from the proximal left ICA and ECA. Which of the following should be included in your preliminary report?

A: There is a probable obstruction of the origin of left carotid artery.
B: Congestive heart failure is most likely present.
C: There is a probable obstruction of the proximal innominate artery.
D: The exam is within normal limits and does not explain the symptoms.
column2:
Answer: A. There is a probable obstruction of the origin of left carotid artery.

Feedback:
The left CCA originates from the aortic arch. Unilaterally damped ECA and ICA waveforms indicate proximal obstruction. Bilaterally reduced resistance and velocity is associated with CHF.

column1:
Which of the following is a responsibility of a Sonographer when assisting with a radiofrequency ablation of the great saphenous vein?

A: to perform initial puncture for the catheter prior to the vascular surgeon entering the room
B: to send the ultrasound catheter for sterilization after the procedure
C: to advance the ultrasound catheter during the procedure
D: to operate the ultrasound machine controls and/or the probe, as directed by the physician
column2:
Answer: D. to operate the ultrasound machine controls and/or the probe, as directed by the physician

Feedback:
The ultrasound catheter cannot be sterilized, it is disposable. The procedure is performed by the physician while the Sonographer assists with the ultrasound machine.

column1:
Which of the following system control adjustments will increase the amount of color Doppler that is superimposed over the 2D image?

A: increased wall filter and increased threshold
B: decreased threshold and persistence
C: decreased persistence and increased wall filter
D: increased threshold and increased persistence
column2:
Answer: D. increased threshold and increased persistence

Feedback:
Threshold Control: AKA color priority; Sets the limits for where color will be displayed over the 2D image; Decreased threshold = decreased color priority; Necessary to minimize color ghosting and improper placement of color on vessel walls Persistence: increasing color persistence will increase the amount of time a color pixel is displayed over an area

column1:
Which calf vessel can be identified between the medial malleolus and the distal portion of the Achilles tendon?

A: posterior tibial artery
B: posterior venous arch
C: peroneal artery
D: anterior tibial artery
column2:
Answer: A. posterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The PTA and PTVs can be identified between the medial malleolus and the distal portion of the Achilles tendon.

column1:
The majority of abdominal aortic aneurysms form in what part of the vessel?

A: Arch
B: Juxtarenal
C: Suprarenal
D: Infrarenal
column2:
Answer: D. Infrarenal

Feedback:
Pararenal, juxtarenal and suprarenal AAA can cause serious complications related to blood flow to the kidneys and renal induced HTN. These types of AAA are considered a critical finding at any size. Infra renal AAA formation is the most common AAA location. Fortunately, this type of AAA can be monitored over time until it reaches 5.5-6cm in size when surgical intervention is necessary.

column1:
_________________________ drain(s) blood from the GSV in the proximal thigh into the proximal and mid femoral vein.

A: Posterior tibial perforators
B: Thigh perforators
C: Paratibial perforators
D: Posterior venous arch
column2:
Answer: B. Thigh perforators

Feedback:
The Hunterian perforators drain blood from the GSV in the proximal/mid-thigh into the femoral vein. They are also referred to as proximal thigh perforators.

column1:
Subcutaneous blood supply to the digits can be evaluated using which non-invasive plethysmography technique?

A: Pulse volume recording
B: Strain gauge plethysmography
C: Photoplethysmography
D: Impedance plethysmography
column2:
Answer: C. Photoplethysmography

Feedback:
Photoplethysmography uses photocells to detect the changes in blood cell volume in subcutaneous tissue during the cardiac cycle.

column1:
Which type of aortic aneurysm is associated with an infection of the arterial wall?

A: Ectatic
B: Mycotic
C: Saccular
D: Berry
column2:
Answer: B. Mycotic

Feedback:
A mycotic aneurysm is caused by infection of the arterial wall which weakens it and allows aneurysm formation.

column1:
You perform a bilateral venous Doppler due to bilateral swelling and pain in the legs. If both external iliac veins look like the tracing on the image, which of the following should be reported on your technologist worksheet?

A: Severe tricuspid regurgitation is suspected
B: May-Thurner syndrome is likely
C: Congestive heart failure is likely
D: IVC obstruction is suspected
column2:
Answer: D. IVC obstruction is suspected

Feedback:
Bilateral continuous waveforms from the iliac veins indicate a proximal obstruction that affects them both. An IVC obstruction is suspected. CHF and tricuspid regurgitation cause cardiac pulsatility in both extremities. May Thurner syndrome leads to thrombosis of the left common iliac vein. Only the left leg would demonstrate continuous flow in the external iliac vein.

column1:
Which of the following is a system adjustment that is made when changing from a lower extremity arterial exam to a lower extremity venous exam on the same patient?

A: decrease Doppler scale
B: reverse the color display baseline
C: decrease transducer frequency
D: change the cursor angle to 30 degrees
column2:
Answer: A. decrease Doppler scale

Feedback:
Venous flow is much lower velocity than arterial flow which requires a lower Doppler scale. The cursor angle does not need to be used/adjusted for venous flow evaluation because flow velocity is not being recorded.

column1:
A patient presents with dysphagia and hoarseness. The referring physician suspects Ortner syndrome. What type of exam will you be performing?

A: transcranial Doppler exam
B: upper extremity arterial duplex exam
C: bilateral carotid duplex exam
D: transcranial Doppler imaging exam
column2:
Answer: B. upper extremity arterial duplex exam

Feedback:
In some patients the right subclavian artery can be a direct branch of the aorta distal to the left subclavian origin. It is called retroesophageal subclavian artery or aberrant right subclavian artery. The artery usually originates from a dilated segment of the proximal descending aorta called the Kommerell diverticulum. The abnormal course of the vessel can cause dysphagia due to compression of the esophagus or the laryngeal nerve. Ortner syndrome - palsy of the recurrent laryngeal nerve that causes changes in the voice, including hoarseness.

column1:
Which of the following arteries are evaluated during a penile duplex exam for erectile dysfunction?

A: urethral
B: ventral
C: dorsal
D: cavernosal
column2:
Answer: D. cavernosal

Feedback:
A penile duplex exam can be performed to evaluate a vascular cause of erectile dysfunction. The cavernosal artery diameters and velocities are evaluated before and after an injection of vasoactive substance. The dorsal vein is also evaluated.

column1:
Significant valvular disease in the right heart usually leads to:

A: tardus parvus waveforms in the carotid arteries
B: exaggerated respiratory phasicity in the portal vein
C: biphasic flow in the carotid arteries
D: cardiac pulsatility in the lower extremity veins
column2:
Answer: D. cardiac pulsatility in the lower extremity veins

Feedback:
Significant valvular disease in the right heart usually leads to significant pulsatility in the lower extremities and portal veins. Significant aortic disease can cause changes to the carotid artery flow. Aortic Stenosis - turbulent flow, post-stenotic flow changes. Aortic Regurgitation - pulsus bisferiens

column1:
A 55yr old with a history of alcoholism, jaundice and cirrhosis is referred for a liver Doppler exam. Which of the following is an expected abnormal finding?

A: respiratory phasicity in portal vein flow
B: multiphasic flow in the hepatic veins
C: dilated IVC
D: increased resistance in the hepatic artery
column2:
Answer: D. increased resistance in the hepatic artery

Feedback:
When an organ has reduced function, it resists the normal amount of blood flow needed. Resistance in the hepatic artery will increase with the increase in resistance to flow in the cirrhotic liver tissue. The coronary vein (AKA left gastric vein) and the umbilical vein will dilate with significant portal HTN. The IVC is not usually affected by isolated cirrhosis. The hepatic vein flow will be multiphasic in the normal patient. Normal portal venous flow demonstrates mild respiratory phasicity.

column1:
Which of the following is a direct branch of the aortic arch?

A: right vertebral
B: left vertebral
C: right carotid
D: left carotid
column2:
Answer: D. left carotid

Feedback:
The three main branches of the aortic arch are the innominate artery, left common carotid artery and left subclavian artery

column1:
A 75yr old female presents for a transcranial doppler evaluation for suspected basilar artery stenosis. She suffers from emphysema and cannot lie down for the exam. Which of the following describes how you can evaluate the basilar artery in this patient?

A: patient in the left lateral decubitus position, hand supporting the head
B: patient seated in chair, lean forward to reach for their toes and flex the neck
C: patient seated in chair, neck hyperextended with a wedge behind the head for support
D: patient seated in chair, neck flexed, and head supported by her arms/hands
column2:
Answer: D. patient seated in chair, neck flexed, and head supported by her arms/hands

Feedback:
If a patient is unable to lie down in the supine or decubitus position for TCD evaluation of the basilar artery, they should be seated in a chair, neck flexed, and head supported by her arms/hands. If the patient is able to lie supine, just not in the decubitus position, then ask them to turn their head away from the side of interest and place the transducer to the left or right side of the foramen magnum

column1:
What is the arrow pointing to on the abdominal angiography exam?

A: normal right renal artery
B: fibromuscular dysplasia of the left renal artery
C: fibromuscular dysplasia of the right renal artery
D: normal tortuous splenic artery
column2:
Answer: C. fibromuscular dysplasia of the right renal artery

Feedback:
FMD demonstrates a bumpy lumen contour like a string of pearls.

column1:
Pulsus bisferiens in the carotid arteries is a sign of:

A: aortic regurgitation
B: mitral regurgitation
C: mitral stenosis
D: pulmonary regurgitation
column2:
Answer: A. aortic regurgitation

Feedback:
Blood moves back through the aortic valve into the left ventricle during diastole. It is usually caused by a diseased valve, congenital defects and chamber or aortic dilatation. Significant regurgitation results in volume overload in the chamber receiving the retrograde blood flow. With aortic regurgitation, volume overload occurs in the left ventricle. Pulsus bisferiens can occur in the carotid arteries. The waveforms will demonstrate double systolic peaks. Severe regurgitation is associated with diastolic flow reversal in the carotid arteries (Water Hammer Pulse).

column1:
An aorto-bifemoral graft is used to treat:

A: bilateral popliteal stenosis
B: unilateral femoral stenosis
C: unilateral iliac stenosis
D: bilateral iliac stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. bilateral iliac stenosis

Feedback:
An aorto-bifemoral graft can be used to correct distal aortic stenosis or bilateral iliac stenosis.

column1:
You are performing an annual carotid duplex on a patient that had a carotid endarterectomy 6 years ago. There is a new layer of homogeneous material lining the ICA and bulb. The Doppler evaluation demonstrates turbulence and elevated velocities. Which of the following is the most likely cause for these findings?

A: neointimal hyperplasia
B: progression of atherosclerotic disease
C: residual blockage from suboptimal surgical techniques
D: intimal flap and dissection
column2:
Answer: B. progression of atherosclerotic disease

Feedback:
Within the first month post-op, restenosis is attributed to suboptimal surgical techniques. Narrowing over the first 24 months after CEA is attributed to neointimal hyperplasia. After 2 years, stenosis occurs due to progression of atherosclerotic disease.

column1:
When venous flow is slow, echogenic particles can be seen near the walls and in the valvular sinus. This phenomenon is called:

A: Virchow triad
B: clutter artifact
C: misregistration artifact
D: spontaneous contrast
column2:
Answer: D. spontaneous contrast

Feedback:
Slow moving red blood cells around the valve leaflets and near the vein walls can be mildly reflective. This phenomenon is called spontaneous contrast.

column1:
Which of the following would be considered abnormal in the Doppler evaluation of a renal transplant?

A: Mildly turbulent flow at the arterial anastomosis site
B: A resistive index of 0.65 for the arcuate arteries
C: Phasic flow in the renal vein
D: Diastolic flow reversal within the parenchymal arteries
column2:
Answer: D. Diastolic flow reversal within the parenchymal arteries

Feedback:
Phasic flow in the renal vein, mildly turbulent flow at the arterial anastomosis site and a resistive index of 0.65 for the arcuate arteries are normal findings in a renal allograft. Loss of diastolic flow or diastolic flow reversal indicates a significant increase in the resistance of the renal vascular beds which is consistent with rejection.

column1:
After an endarterectomy or carotid stent placement, the first follow up exam should be performed:

A: immediately following the procedure
B: within 5 days of the procedure
C: on the 1-year anniversary date of the procedure
D: within one month after the procedure
column2:
Answer: D. within one month after the procedure

Feedback:
A patient that has had an endarterectomy or carotid stent placement is usually evaluated within the first month following the procedure. If performed too soon after the endarterectomy or other open surgical procedure, artifacts produced by air in the patch or graft may inhibit the exam.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes this image?

A: The image is incorrectly labelled, but the diameter measurement is performed correctly.
B: The image is incorrectly labelled, and the diameter measurement is performed incorrectly.
C: The image is correctly labelled, and the diameter measurement is performed correctly.
D: The image is correctly labelled, but the diameter measurement is performed incorrectly.
column2:
Answer: B. The image is incorrectly labelled, and the diameter measurement is performed incorrectly.

Feedback:
The image is incorrectly labelled, and the diameter measurement is performed incorrectly. The measurement is being obtained above the celiac and SMA origin. This indicates the proximal aorta segment. The aorta should be measured from outer wall to outer wall for an accurate diameter. This image demonstrates the outer wall to inner wall diameter.

column1:
A diabetic patient presents for extremity arterial evaluation with a rest pain bilaterally and blackened tips of the 4th and 5th toe on the left foot. Right Ankle 148mmHg Left Ankle 144mmHg Highest brachial pressure 140mmHg. Which statement is true regarding these findings?

A: The signs and symptoms are most likely related to associated venous insufficiency.
B: Toe pressures should be obtained for further evaluation.
C: An exercise arterial exam is necessary to properly diagnose the patient.
D: The signs and symptoms are most likely related to dehydration and poor hygiene.
column2:
Answer: B. Toe pressures should be obtained for further evaluation.

Feedback:
Medial calcification does not always cause an excessive ABI measurement of greater than 1.30. The symptoms of significant ischemia do not fit the normal ABI measurements. The diabetic history also adds to the risk for medial calcification. Toe pressures and/or PPG or PVR evaluation will provide better information on this patient.

column1:
A patient presents with a script for a "vascular ultrasound" due to a history of Paget Schroetter syndrome. What type of exam should be performed for this patient?

A: segmental pressures on the lower extremities
B: arterial PPG of the toes
C: upper extremity venous Doppler exam
D: lower extremity venous Doppler exam
column2:
Answer: C. upper extremity venous Doppler exam

Feedback:
Paget Schroetter syndrome refers to the formation of upper extremity DVT due to excessive motion. It is usually associated with thoracic outlet syndrome.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes Hollenhorst plaques?

A: Most patients experience intermittent periods of unilateral blindness
B: Heavily calcified plaque that forms in the aortic arch
C: Usually seen on optometry exams
D: Heavily calcified plaque that forms in the distal aorta
column2:
Answer: C. Usually seen on optometry exams

Feedback:
Hollenhorst plaques are cholesterol emboli that originate from atherosclerotic lesions in the ipsilateral carotid artery or aorta and lodge in the small branches of the retinal artery. They are usually identified by an optometrist on a standard vision exam because the patient is asymptomatic.

column1:
_______________ reflux will be normally seen in the lower extremity superficial system.

A: No (0s)
B: Less than 0.5s
C: Less than 1.5s
D: Less than 3s
column2:
Answer: B. Less than 0.5s

Feedback:
A small of amount of reflux (Less than 0.5s) may be seen in normal vessels with flow normalization following augmentation.

column1:
Which of the following can lead to an increase in the frequency shift detected in the common femoral artery?

A: Proximal superficial femoral artery occlusion
B: 80% stenosis of the superficial femoral artery
C: Decreased probe frequency
D: 80% stenosis of the common femoral artery
column2:
Answer: D. 80% stenosis of the common femoral artery

Feedback:
Stenosis in the CFA will increase the detected frequency shift (and calculated velocity). Using a higher frequency probe will increase the detected frequency shift. Significant stenosis or occlusion of the SFA will reduce the detected frequency shift (velocity) in the CFA.

column1:
Which of the following describes a positive result from the Adson maneuver?

A: loss of the radial artery pulse
B: loss of pedal pulses
C: increased hydrostatic pressure
D: pain in the foot with quick dorsiflexion
column2:
Answer: A. loss of the radial artery pulse

Feedback:
The Adson maneuver is a patient position used to evaluate thoracic outlet syndrome. If the radial pulse diminishes or disappears completely during the maneuver, TOS is diagnosed.

column1:
Which of the following is considered a normal finding in a patient with a brachiobasilic hemodialysis graft?

A: Triphasic flow in the brachial artery proximal to the graft and monophasic flow in the brachial artery distal to the graft
B: Dilated brachial artery proximal to the graft with low resistance monophasic flow
C: Continuous flow in the basilic vein proximal and distal to the graft anastomosis
D: Increased resistance and velocity with a loss of flow in diastole in the brachial artery proximal and distal to the graft
column2:
Answer: B. Dilated brachial artery proximal to the graft with low resistance monophasic flow

Feedback:
The connection between the artery and the vein will cause the resistance in the brachial artery to decrease and become monophasic. The basilic vein will exhibit pulsatile, high velocity venous flow in the area of the graft due to the arterial inflow.

column1:
Which of the following describes cerebral cross-over collateralization?

A: flow from the right MCA to the left MCA through a cross over collateral vessel
B: flow from the left ACA to the right ACA through the anterior communicating artery
C: flow from the left MCA to the right MCA through a cross over collateral vessel
D: left ICA flow through posterior communicator to the left vertebral
column2:
Answer: B. flow from the left ACA to the right ACA through the anterior communicating artery

Feedback:
Cross over collateralization occurs when flow from one side of the brain crosses over midline to feed the opposite side. If an ICA is occluded, flow moves from the contralateral ICA into the contralateral ACA and then through anterior communicator into the ipsilateral ACA. An example would be: Right ICA occlusion = flow from the left ACA moves into the right ACA through the anterior communicating artery. Flow toward the transducer in the ACA indicates ipsilateral ICA obstruction

column1:
You are evaluating a patient that had a balloon angioplasty on the proximal superficial femoral artery 3 years ago. The acceleration time in the common femoral artery is 230ms. This finding is most suggestive of:

A: normal arterial inflow
B: obstruction in the distal superficial femoral artery
C: iliac artery stenosis
D: recurrence of stenosis in the proximal superficial artery
column2:
Answer: C. iliac artery stenosis

Feedback:
Increased AT = longer time to reach peak velocity = lower resistance = proximal obstruction Decreased AT = less time to reach peak velocity = higher resistance = distal obstruction Increased acceleration time indicates a proximal stenosis. There is probably an iliac artery stenosis and the arteries proximal to the CFA should be evaluated. If the AT in the CFA was shorter or decreased, this indicates a stenosis distally. In this scenario the femoral artery may have restenosis or the distal SFA could have stenosis.

column1:
A segmental pressure exam is performed for a patient who is experiencing rest pain in the right leg. An ABI of 0.42 was found on the right leg. Which of the following statements is true regarding the findings?

A: A PVR exam will provide additional diagnostic information for this patient.
B: Exercise testing will provide additional diagnostic information for this patient.
C: The patient will be referred to a vascular surgeon for intervention.
D: A tourniquet will be applied to the upper thigh and the ankle pressure repeated.
column2:
Answer: C. The patient will be referred to a vascular surgeon for intervention.

Feedback:
There is significant disease present. Exercise testing or a pulse volume recording exam will not provide additional information that will aid the physician in diagnosis and treatment. The patient is already a candidate for surgical intervention.

column1:
__________________________________ refers to a collection of mucinous material within the adventitial wall layer of the affected vessel

A: Mucinous dissection
B: Vasa vasorum
C: Adventitial cyst
D: Baker cyst
column2:
Answer: C. Adventitial cyst

Feedback:
Adventitial cysts are a collection of mucinous material within the adventitial wall layer of the affected vessel. The cysts predominantly form in the walls of the peripheral arteries, with the popliteal artery most commonly affected. It is most common in young to middle-aged men with no other vascular disorders. Usually presents as multiple anechoic or hypoechoic areas within the wall. No blood flow is detected in the areas. The cysts can cause narrowing of the vessel lumen and stenosis. The compression of the artery is described as creating the appearance of a Scimitar knife.

column1:
Normal perforator valves allow blood flow in the perforator to move:

A: from the GSV to femoral vein
B: toward the heart
C: from the femoral vein to the GSV
D: toward the ankle
column2:
Answer: A. from the GSV to femoral vein

Feedback:
Normal perforator valves allow blood flow in the perforator to move from the superficial system to the deep system. Dilated perforators will allow bidirectional blood blow.

column1:
Which of the following describes the sonographic appearance of a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt?

A: strongly reflective curved structure connecting the right portal vein and right hepatic vein
B: shunts are not easily evaluated Sonographically and CTA is the preferred method for evaluation
C: requires color Doppler evaluation to be able to visualize the graft within the liver
D: anechoic tube, without distinctive wall reflection, which connects the right hepatic vein to the right portal vein
column2:
Answer: A. strongly reflective curved structure connecting the right portal vein and right hepatic vein

Feedback:
The shunt is constructed of metallic substances with increased reflectivity in order to allow for better Sonographic visualization. The sonographic appearance of a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt is a strongly reflective curved structure connecting the right portal vein and right hepatic vein.

column1:
The most common reason for underestimation of arterial stenosis is:

A: improper sample volume location
B: insonation of a vein and artery simultaneously
C: improper sample volume angle
D: insonation of a vein instead of an artery
column2:
Answer: A. improper sample volume location

Feedback:
The most common reason for underestimation of arterial stenosis is improper sample volume location. Anytime stenosis is suspected, the cursor should be walked through the area of stenosis to locate the area of highest velocity. Lack of proper sample placement can cause you to miss the area of highest velocity and therefore underestimate the stenosis.

column1:
A patient presents with left lower extremity pain that awakens him at night. The pain is relieved by sitting at the side of the bed for a few minutes. What would the predicted ABI value be for the left leg?

A: 0.8
B: 0.6
C: 0.4
D: above 1.0; these symptoms are most likely unrelated to arterial disease
column2:
Answer: C. 0.4

Feedback:
The patient is experiencing rest pain which is found in patients with an ABI less than 0.5

column1:
An aortic dissection most commonly originates:

A: in the descending thoracic aorta, just distal to the origin of the left subclavian artery
B: in the infrarenal descending aorta
C: in the aortic isthmus
D: in the ascending aorta
column2:
Answer: D. in the ascending aorta

Feedback:
Aortic dissection most commonly occurs in the ascending aorta. The descending thoracic aorta, just distal to the origin of the left subclavian artery is the second most common site for dissection.

column1:
A patient presents for a lower extremity venous Doppler due to leg pain in the groin and upper thigh after a recent car accident. After viewing these findings in the common femoral vein, what should you do next on this patient's exam?

A: evaluate the common femoral artery for signs of an AV fistula
B: evaluate the common femoral artery for a pseudoaneurysm
C: perform a blood pressure on the thigh
D: perform the color and Doppler evaluation of the superficial femoral vein to rule out thrombus
column2:
Answer: A. evaluate the common femoral artery for signs of an AV fistula

Feedback:
Note the pulsatility in the venous waveform which is consistent with the presence of arterial inflow proximally in the vessel. The mosaic color pattern indicates turbulence at the connection with the artery.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid Doppler exam due to left sided amaurosis fugax. These findings are most suggestive of stenosis in:

A: left vertebral artery
B: left ICA
C: right ICA
D: right vertebral artery
column2:
Answer: B. left ICA

Feedback:
Visual symptoms occur on the same side as the stenosis because the ophthalmic artery is a branch of the distal ICA.

column1:
A mesenteric to aortic ratio is considered normal when it is ________________.

A: >1.0
B: 1.0 or less
C: >3.0
D: <3.0
column2:
Answer: B. 1.0 or less

Feedback:
The mesenteric velocity should be similar to the aortic velocity, which would yield a ratio of around 1.0. As the stenosis in the SMA increases, the velocity increases and the ratio increases. SMA/aorta ratio >3.0 indicates significant stenosis.

column1:
When performing a photoplethysmography exam for venous insufficiency, where should you place the sensor?

A: medial distal calf
B: groin
C: dorsal aspect of the great toe
D: distal pad of the great toe
column2:
Answer: A. medial distal calf

Feedback:
Venous Insufficiency is assessed by placing the sensor on the distal calf. Arterial disease is assessed by placing the sensor on the pads of the toes.

column1:
What is the primary purpose of using a tourniquet during a venous PPG exam of the leg?

A: to evaluate the perforators for reflux
B: to determine if thrombus is confined to the superficial system
C: to determine if thrombus is present in the deep or superficial system
D: to determine if venous insufficiency is confined to the superficial system
column2:
Answer: D. to determine if venous insufficiency is confined to the superficial system

Feedback:
When the venous refill time (VRT) is less than 20 seconds, a tourniquet is applied to the leg at the groin to occlude superficial venous flow. The VRT is re-evaluated and if it is now >20 seconds, the insufficiency is only present in the superficial system. If the VRT does not return to normal, the insufficiency is in the deep system (and possibly the superficial system).

column1:
Which of the following techniques cannot be used to monitor distal flow in the leg while compression therapy for a pseudoaneurysm is performed?

A: Pressure monitor on the great toe
B: PPG sensor on the great toe
C: Doppler evaluation of the PTA or DPA
D: PPG sensor on the ankle
column2:
Answer: D. PPG sensor on the ankle

Feedback:
When PPG is used to evaluate arterial flow, the sensors are placed on the great toes. When PPG is used to evaluate venous reflux, the sensor is placed on the lower calf near the ankle. It is contraindicated to obtain an ankle pressure in a patient with an acute pseudoaneurysm. A toe monitor can be used because the inflation of the cuff has little to no effect on pressure in the femoral artery.

column1:
Which exam would be improved by the administration of Simethicone prior to the ultrasound evaluation?

A: renal artery duplex
B: lower extremity segmental pressures
C: transcranial duplex
D: carotid duplex
column2:
Answer: A. renal artery duplex

Feedback:
Simethicone is a medication that is used to reduce bowel gas. It may be beneficial when performing abdominal duplex exams.

column1:
You receive an order for a pre-op arterial mapping to evaluate the internal mammary artery. What vessel should you evaluate to locate the origin of the internal mammary artery?

A: innominate artery
B: descending aorta
C: aortic arch
D: subclavian artery
column2:
Answer: D. subclavian artery

Feedback:
The internal mammary artery (AKA internal thoracic artery) originates from the subclavian artery.

column1:
What measurement is being taken in the image?

A: IMT
B: Continuity equation
C: Diameter stenosis
D: Area stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. Area stenosis

Feedback:
Area stenosis is calculated by measuring the area of the vessel and the true lumen. Area 1 minus Area 2 divided by Area 1 X 100% EX: 4cm2 - 2.5cm2 / 4cm2 = 0.375 = 38% stenosis

column1:
In most patients, the first branch of the ECA is:

A: the ophthalmic artery
B: the superior thyroidal artery
C: the superficial temporal artery
D: the internal thoracic artery
column2:
Answer: B. the superior thyroidal artery

Feedback:
In most patients, the first branch of the ECA is the superior thyroidal artery. The internal thoracic artery is a branch of the subclavian artery. The superficial temporal artery is a branch of the ECA but more distally. The ophthalmic artery is a branch of the ICA.

column1:
Which of the following parameters is displayed as the y-axis on a Doppler tracing?

A: time
B: frequency shift
C: speed
D: motion
column2:
Answer: B. frequency shift

Feedback:
Doppler shift frequencies are displayed on a tracing as a function of time; x axis = time, y axis = Doppler shift; Velocity could also be a correct answer, but speed is incorrect. Speed does not indicate the direction of the motion. Doppler evaluation provides the speed and direction of the flow = velocity.

column1:
The ability of PW Doppler to determine the _____________________________ is the primary advantage over CW Doppler techniques.

A: location of stenosis in the vessel
B: pulsatility of flow
C: direction of flow
D: velocity of flow
column2:
Answer: A. location of stenosis in the vessel

Feedback:
CW Doppler provides no depth resolution during Doppler evaluation. CW Doppler is preferred for higher velocities and deeper flow evaluations. PW Doppler is able to provide velocity, pulsatility and location of a stenosis.

column1:
The hepatic artery carries _______ of the blood entering the liver.

A: 20-30%
B: 50-60%
C: 80-90%
D: 100%
column2:
Answer: A. 20-30%

Feedback:
The portal vein carries 70-80% of the blood entering the liver, but it is lower in oxygen saturation at 85%. The hepatic artery carries 20-30% of the blood into the liver and it has a 95% oxygen saturation. The portal venous system supplies just over half of the oxygen to the liver tissue because of the high volume of flow. The hepatic artery supplies just under half of the oxygen to the liver tissues.

column1:
Where will you place the transducer to evaluate flow in the posterior tibial artery?

A: posterior to the medial malleolus
B: posterior to the lateral malleolus
C: on the foot, between the 1st and 2nd digit
D: anterior to the medial malleolus
column2:
Answer: A. posterior to the medial malleolus

Feedback:
To evaluate flow in the PTA, the transducer is placed posterior to the medial malleolus. The PTA courses between the medial malleolus and the Achilles tendon.

column1:
Once the pressure in the deep venous system consistently exceeds the pressure in the superficial system,

A: perforators will dilate and allow flow to only move into the deep system
B: perforators will constrict forcing flow reversal into the superficial system
C: perforators will exhibit stasis of flow and thrombus formation
D: perforators will dilate leading to bi-directional flow
column2:
Answer: D. perforators will dilate leading to bi-directional flow

Feedback:
Once the pressure in the deep venous system consistently exceeds the pressure in the superficial system, perforators will dilate leading to bi-directional flow.

column1:
The lateral plantar arch artery originates at the _____________________.

A: anterior tibial artery
B: peroneal artery
C: popliteal artery
D: posterior tibial artery
column2:
Answer: D. posterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The lateral plantar arch originates at the PTA.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes a positive result for a segmental pressure and PPG examination performed to rule out Dialysis Access Steal Syndrome?

A: 0.9 Digital/Brachial Index on the resting baseline pressure evaluation
B: Increased amplitude of the digital PPG tracings during compression of the outflow vein
C: Decreased Digital/Brachial Index during compression of the outflow vein
D: Absent flow demonstrated on the digital PPG tracings with compression of the outflow vein
column2:
Answer: B. Increased amplitude of the digital PPG tracings during compression of the outflow vein

Feedback:
Positive segmental pressure/PPG exam for Dialysis Access Steal Syndrome: Increased amplitude of the digital PPG tracings during compression of the outflow vein <0.7 Digital/Brachial Index on the resting baseline pressure evaluation Increased Digital/Brachial Index during compression of the outflow vein The increased amplitude and distal pressure values occur due to restoration of normal flow with outflow vein compression.

column1:
Which of the following is a contraindication for exercise testing?

A: Non-compressible calf arteries
B: Systemic HTN with systolic pressure >200mmHg
C: History of cardiac procedure or myocardial infarction
D: More than one of the above
column2:
Answer: D. More than one of the above

Feedback:
Contraindications for Exercise Testing: Known disease in one extremity that is untreated Inability to walk without assistance History of stroke with residual paralysis Shortness of breath Low exercise tolerance Systemic HTN with systolic pressure >200mmHg Noncompressible calf arteries Unstable angina and/or use of nitroglycerin for chest pain History of cardiac procedure or myocardial infarction - must be cleared for exercise testing by cardiologist

column1:
A varicose vein is usually:

A: a dilated vessel of the perforator system.
B: a dilated tributary of the greater saphenous vein.
C: a dilated lesser saphenous vein.
D: a dilated tributary of the deep system.
column2:
Answer: B. a dilated tributary of the greater saphenous vein.

Feedback:
The greater saphenous vein and associated tributaries are most commonly associated with varicosity formation.

column1:
Which of the following describes how to correctly measure the AP diameter of iliac artery?

A: transverse view, outer wall to outer wall
B: longitudinal view, outer wall to inner wall
C: transverse view, outer wall to inner wall
D: longitudinal view, outer wall to outer wall
column2:
Answer: D. longitudinal view, outer wall to outer wall

Feedback:
When evaluating the diameter of the aorta and iliac arteries, AP Dimensions are obtained in longitudinal plane, from outer wall to outer wall of the vessel. Width Dimensions are obtained in the transverse plane, from outer wall to outer wall of the vessel.

column1:
If the systolic brachial pressure is 116mmHg, what is the minimum toe pressure that would be considered normal?

A: 80mmHg
B: 60mmHg
C: 100mmHg
D: 70mmHg
column2:
Answer: D. 70mmHg

Feedback:
The normal pressure in the lower extremity digits is normally at least 60% of the brachial pressure. 116 x 0.60 = 70mmHg. The toe pressure is considered normal if it is 70mmHg or higher.

column1:
Which of the following is true regarding the Doppler tracing of the SMA?

A: normal SMA flow pattern pre-prandially
B: normal SMA flow pattern post-prandially
C: SMA stenosis
D: There is a pseudoaneurysm of the SMA causing very turbulent flow.
column2:
Answer: C. SMA stenosis

Feedback:
The peak velocity of the flow is over 2.75m/s and there is a high level of diastolic flow which indicates a stenosis greater than 75%.

column1:
The transducer is placed in the longitudinal position slightly anterior to midline of the medial upper left calf. The beam is angled posterior until the image displayed appears. What vessel is indicated by letter A?

A: posterior tibial vein
B: peroneal vein
C: anterior tibial vein
D: gastrocnemius vein
column2:
Answer: A. posterior tibial vein

Feedback:
The image displays the confluence of the posterior tibial and peroneal veins. The peroneal veins are deeper on the image.

column1:
Vasoconstriction will cause ________________ , while vasodilation will cause ______________.

A: increased resistance to flow, decreased resistance to flow
B: increased flow rate, decreased flow rate
C: increased heart rate, increased blood pressure
D: increased flow volume, decreased flow volume
column2:
Answer: A. increased resistance to flow, decreased resistance to flow

Feedback:
Vasoconstriction in the arterioles will cause increased resistance to flow in the feeding vessels, while vasodilation will cause reduced resistance.

column1:
If there is a left subclavian steal, which brachial artery evaluation will be abnormal?

A: Left brachial will have a higher BP and a triphasic waveform
B: Right brachial will have a lower BP and a blunted/monophasic waveform
C: Right brachial will have a higher BP and a blunted/monophasic waveform
D: Left brachial will have a lower BP and a blunted/monophasic waveform
column2:
Answer: D. Left brachial will have a lower BP and a blunted/monophasic waveform

Feedback:
If there is a left sided steal, this indicates the left subclavian artery has an occlusion at the origin. The left vertebral artery originates from the left subclavian artery, just distal to its origin from the arch. The left vertebral artery now receives no antegrade flow toward the head, due to the subclavian blockage. Blood flows toward the head in the right vertebral artery to join the left vertebral artery to form the basilar artery. Some flow from the right vertebral reverses into the left vertebral artery to feed the left subclavian artery and arm. The BP in the left arm drops due to the blood flow coming in from the low resistance, cerebral circulation. The distal arm pressure will decrease to allow flow to enter the "empty" vessels above, in a retrograde fashion. Vertebral flow is low resistance and monophasic. The Doppler evaluation of the left arm will demonstrate these characteristics which are abnormal for an extremity.

column1:
The subclavian artery becomes the axillary artery:

A: when it crosses over the deltoid muscle
B: at the level of the fourth rib
C: when it crosses anterior to the clavicle
D: at the level of the first rib
column2:
Answer: D. at the level of the first rib

Feedback:
The subclavian artery extends from the innominate artery to the axillary artery. It courses posterior to the anterior scalene muscle and clavicle. It becomes the axillary artery at the level of the first rib.

column1:
What is the proper patient position and respiration for assessing the portal vein diameter?

A: supine with inspiration and the Valsalva strain
B: supine with quiet respiration
C: right lateral decubitus position with deep inspiration
D: left lateral decubitus position with deep inspiration
column2:
Answer: B. supine with quiet respiration

Feedback:
The portal vein should be assessed with the patient supine and with quiet respiration. The measurement should be obtained at the point the portal vein crosses the IVC.

column1:
What are the standard windows used for a complete transcranial Doppler exam?

A: suboccipital, transtemporal windows
B: suboccipital, transtemporal, transorbital, and submandibular windows
C: suboccipital, transtemporal, and periorbital windows
D: transtemporal window only, other views performed if abnormalities identified
column2:
Answer: B. suboccipital, transtemporal, transorbital, and submandibular windows

Feedback:
