The four standard windows used for a TCD exam are the suboccipital, transtemporal, transorbital/ophthalmic, and submandibular windows. The periorbital window is a supplemental view used to assess the frontal artery (branch of the ophthalmic artery).

column1:
If the Doppler cursor angle is underestimated, the velocity of the flow will be ____________________.

A: overestimated
B: be unaffected
C: dependent solely on the strength of the frequency shift
D: underestimated
column2:
Answer: D. underestimated

Feedback:
The Doppler angle of incidence is the angle at which the Doppler beam intersects the blood flow. The cursor angle is aligned with the vessel walls. If this angle is underestimated, the velocity of the flow will be underestimated. If this angle is overestimated, the velocity of flow will be overestimated.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the IVC and click to mark the structure.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The IVC is posterior to the caudate lobe of the liver.

column1:
If a renal artery Doppler exam demonstrates normal flow and normal flow is identified on angiography evaluation, the US results are described as:

A: true negative
B: false positive
C: false negative
D: true positive
column2:
Answer: A. true negative

Feedback:
If the current exam is negative but the gold standard is positive = false negative results on current exam If the current exam is positive but the gold standard is negative= false positive results on current exam If the current exam is negative and the gold standard is negative = true negative results on current exam If the current exam is positive and the gold standard is positive = true positive results on current exam

column1:
How many cuffs are recommended when performing a bilateral lower extremity segmental pressure evaluation?

A: 3
B: 4
C: 5
D: 8
column2:
Answer: D. 8

Feedback:
The most common protocol for a segmental pressure exam of both of the legs involves 4 cuffs per leg for a total of 8.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid ultrasound. The exam demonstrates significant atheroma formation and string flow in the proximal right ICA. Which of the following signs or symptoms listed in the patient chart would be related to the findings?

A: right sided hemiparesis
B: right sided bruit
C: left eye visual disturbances
D: left arm and leg paresthesia
column2:
Answer: D. left arm and leg paresthesia

Feedback:
A critical stenosis that produces only minimal flow at the stenosis will not demonstrate a bruit. The right side of the brain controls the left side of the body. Left side symptoms in the body will be associated with right ICA disease. Visual symptoms occur on the same side as the obstruction. Left eye symptoms will be associated with left ICA disease.

column1:
Which of the following non-stimulated PPG tracings is expected in a patient with Raynaud syndrome?

A: none of the tracings are consistent with Raynaud's syndrome
B: A
C: B
D: C
column2:
Answer: D. C

Feedback:
A PPG tracing from a non-stimulated Raynaud patient will have a slower than normal upstroke and delayed downslope BUT there will be an early peak of the waveform.

column1:
If a patient complains of a recent onset of uncontrollable systemic HTN with average BP readings exceeding 170/90mmHg, what vascular cause should be suspected?

A: portal HTN
B: renal artery stenosis
C: hepatic congestion
D: subclavian stenosis
column2:
Answer: B. renal artery stenosis

Feedback:
A sudden increase in systemic BP with no response to medication is usually associated with renovascular HTN caused by renal artery stenosis.

column1:
What is the expected waveform characteristic if the Doppler sample volume is placed in the mid ICA?

A: peaked waveform with decreased peak systolic and end diastolic velocities
B: late peaking, low velocity, low resistance waveform
C: increased peak systolic velocity and decreased end diastolic velocity
D: biphasic flow with increased peak systolic and end diastolic velocities
column2:
Answer: B. late peaking, low velocity, low resistance waveform

Feedback:
The calculated diameter stenosis is 76% which indicates a severe stenosis. The waveform at the stenosis will indicate a monophasic waveform with increased systolic and diastolic flow pattern. A sample taken distal to the stenosis in the mid ICA will demonstrate a blunted late peaking, low velocity, low resistance waveform.

column1:
Which two cursors would be used to measure the velocities used to calculate the resistive index for the flow in this renal artery tracing?

A: red and blue
B: red and green
C: blue and yellow
D: yellow and red
column2:
Answer: C. blue and yellow

Feedback:
The AT is measured by placing a cursor at the start of the systolic upstroke and at the early systolic peak (if present). The velocities used to calculate the RI are measured by placing the cursor at the true peak systolic velocity and the end diastolic velocity.

column1:
A patient presents with a history of right hip and thigh pain with numbness when he walks. The exercise time varies before the pain starts and it is relieved by sitting down. There are no symptoms in his lower leg. These findings are most suggestive of?

A: Raynaud's disease
B: chronic venous disease
C: peripheral arterial disease
D: neurogenic cause
column2:
Answer: D. neurogenic cause

Feedback:
The leg pain is not consistent and reproducible which are key components of claudication related to vascular disease. Neurogenic claudication is related to nerve compression proximal to the symptoms. The feeling of pain and paresthesia is similar to that with arterial disease, but the exercise time varies before the symptoms occur. The symptoms experienced with arterial disease are in the distal leg, while symptoms confined to the upper leg are typically related to nerve compression.

column1:
If a patient is diagnosed with a pulmonary embolism, what should be the next step in their patient care?

A: IV Heparin
B: lower extremity venous Doppler exam
C: V/Q scan
D: pulmonary angiography
column2:
Answer: A. IV Heparin

Feedback:
If the patient has been diagnosed with a PE, additional exams may or may not be required to locate the source of the embolism. Immediate IV Heparin therapy should be administered.

column1:
A patient presents for pre-operative arterial mapping for the TRAM flap breast reconstruction procedure. What vessel(s) should be evaluated?

A: epigastric arteries and radial artery
B: internal mammary artery and radial artery
C: radial artery
D: epigastric and internal mammary arteries
column2:
Answer: D. epigastric and internal mammary arteries

Feedback:
Internal Mammary Artery: AKA internal thoracic artery originates from subclavian artery; Used in breast reconstruction and as coronary graft for left anterior descending (LAD) artery Epigastric Arteries: Superior EA originates from internal mammary artery and shares anastomoses with the inferior EA; Inferior EA originates from the external iliac artery; Both vessels supply the rectus abdominis muscle used in TRAM Flap procedure for breast reconstruction; Evaluated to locate the section of muscle with the best perfusion for surgery

column1:
If chronic occlusion is suspected and the artery is not easily identifiable because of atrophy, what is the best way to locate the artery of interest?

A: use the PPG sensor and move it around the area of interest to locate an arterial pulse
B: use color Doppler to locate flow in the adjacent paired vein
C: use Power Doppler to locate areas of perfusion of the muscles and trace that flow back to the main artery
D: use PW Doppler to search the surrounding tissues for an arterial waveform
column2:
Answer: B. use color Doppler to locate flow in the adjacent paired vein

Feedback:
If chronic occlusion is suspected and the artery is not easily identifiable, use color Doppler to locate flow in the adjacent vein. Most arteries in the extremities have an accompanying vein. A calcified, atrophied artery can be identified coursing adjacent to its paired vein.

column1:
The red arrows on the image are pointing to what abnormal characteristic of the Doppler waveform?

A: Temporal tap response
B: Formation of a spectral window
C: Vibrations from post stenotic turbulence
D: Crosstalk artifact
column2:
Answer: C. Vibrations from post stenotic turbulence

Feedback:
The arrows indicate varied flow velocities seen in post-stenotic turbulence. Note the multiple and numerous velocity spikes throughout the tracing and the mosaic color displayed on the guiding image. The ICA does not typically respond to the temporal tap maneuver. The rate of spiking would not be possible with normal tapping for the maneuver.

column1:
Which of the following is an important part of Sonographer - Patient communication?

A: verifying the patient's name and date of birth
B: explaining the exam and the results
C: offering advice on ways to alleviate symptoms until the doctor treats the problem
D: asking the patient if they are allergic to iodine
column2:
Answer: A. verifying the patient's name and date of birth

Feedback:
Important parts of sonographer Communication include verifying the patient's name and date of birth, explaining the exam and when to expect the results and helping the patient locate their next exam or the exit. Sonographers should not offer treatment advice to the patient. Always refer them to their doctor when they have questions about their health status. Iodinated contrast is used radiology procedures. If the patient is having that type of procedure, the technologist performing the exam will assess their history.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the negative predictive value (NPV) of a testing technique?

A: The ability of a test to rule out disease when it IS NOT present.
B: The ability of a test to detect disease when it IS present.
C: If the test result is positive, NPV is the probability that the patient actually has the disease
D: If the test result is negative, NPV is the probability that the patient does not have disease
column2:
Answer: D. If the test result is negative, NPV is the probability that the patient does not have disease

Feedback:
Negative Predictive Value-Calculates how often a negative study is correctly diagnosed; If the test result is negative NPV is the probability that the patient does not have disease.

column1:
Which of the following is not a characteristic of a normal PVR tracing of the lower extremity?

A: dicrotic notch
B: anacrotic limb
C: peaked amplitude
D: diastolic flow reversal
column2:
Answer: D. diastolic flow reversal

Feedback:
PVR waveforms do not have a flow reversal component. The tracing represents volume which never goes into the negative.

column1:
Which of the following can lead to systolic flow reversal in the hepatic veins?

A: portal HTN
B: Budd Chiari Syndrome
C: IVC thrombus at the level of the renal veins
D: significant tricuspid regurgitation
column2:
Answer: D. significant tricuspid regurgitation

Feedback:
Tricuspid regurgitation is blood flow that falls back into the right atrium. It can cause the right atrium to dilate and the chamber pressure to increase. If the pressure in the RA increases, inflow from the IVC (and hepatic veins) will be disrupted and flow reversal in systole can occur.

column1:
What is demonstrated on the longitudinal image taken from the mid-thigh?

A: occlusion of the deep femoral artery
B: occlusion of the superficial femoral artery
C: chronic occlusive thrombosis of the femoral vein
D: chronic occlusive thrombosis of the profunda femoral vein
column2:
Answer: C. chronic occlusive thrombosis of the femoral vein

Feedback:
Note the increased echogenicity of the thrombus and the contracted vein which are consistent with chronic venous thrombosis. There is an area in the distal vein where the thrombus has contracted and hardened enough to produce posterior shadowing on the image.

column1:
Which of the following is a contraindication for endovenous laser ablation of the great saphenous vein?

A: Baker cyst in the ipsilateral popliteal fossa
B: chronic obstruction of the femoral vein
C: valvular incompetence in the great saphenous vein
D: chronic obstruction of the peroneal veins
column2:
Answer: B. chronic obstruction of the femoral vein

Feedback:
IT IS VERY IMPORTANT to evaluate the venous outflow system of a patient prior to removal or closure of the saphenous vein. If the deep system is obstructed and the superficial system is the main outflow for the leg, the saphenous vein cannot be removed/closed.

column1:
Treadmill exercise is normally performed:

A: until symptoms are intolerable
B: up to 12 minutes or until symptoms are intolerable
C: up to 5 minutes
D: until symptoms are intolerable or up to 5 minutes
column2:
Answer: D. until symptoms are intolerable or up to 5 minutes

Feedback:
Treadmill exercise is normally performed until symptoms are intolerable or up to 5 minutes.

column1:
When transmural pressure in a vein is high:

A: the associated artery will distend and flow velocity decreases
B: the vein becomes enlarged and rounded
C: the associated artery will flatten and flow velocity increases
D: the vein becomes flattened and dumbbell shaped
column2:
Answer: B. the vein becomes enlarged and rounded

Feedback:
Transmural pressure is the pressure within the veins pushing outward and is normally low. It increases with increasing venous volume. When the pressure inside the vein is high compared to the surrounding pressure outside the vein, the vessel distends. When the pressure inside the vein is low compared to the surrounding pressure outside the vein, the vessel collapses to a dumbbell shape.

column1:
A proximal femoral artery stenosis will cause the __________________ in the mid femoral artery.

A: acceleration time to decrease
B: velocity to increase
C: acceleration time to increase
D: Reynold's number to decrease
column2:
Answer: C. acceleration time to increase

Feedback:
Acceleration time refers to the time from the start of systole to peak systole. High resistance vessels have short acceleration times. Low resistance vessels and areas distal to a stenosis will demonstrate longer acceleration times.

column1:
Which of the following describes internalization of the ECA flow?

A: The ECA will have a low resistance Doppler flow pattern similar to the waveform in a normal ICA.
B: Anatomic variation of the ECA where it is found within the cerebral tissues and connects to the circle of Willis, similar to the distal ICA.
C: The left ECA will have a high resistance Doppler flow pattern similar to the ICA.
D: The ECA and ICA are transposed anatomically. The ICA has many branches with a smaller caliber and the ECA is larger with no visible branches.
column2:
Answer: A. The ECA will have a low resistance Doppler flow pattern similar to the waveform in a normal ICA.

Feedback:
The conversion of the ECA flow to more of an ICA flow pattern can occur with ICA occlusion and ECA collateral formation. If the ECA takes over the supply of the cerebral tissues, the flow within becomes low resistance with increased diastolic flow to accommodate the new organ that is now being supplied.

column1:
You are performing a TCD exam to assess collateral flow with a right ICA occlusion. You identify flow moving away from the transducer at 90cm/s with decreased pulsatility in the right ophthalmic artery. The left ophthalmic artery demonstrates flow moving toward the transducer with a velocity of 45cm/s. What is indicated by these findings?

A: Posterior to anterior collateralization
B: Cross over collateralization
C: ECA to ICA collateralization
D: Normal flow parameters, no collateralization present
column2:
Answer: C. ECA to ICA collateralization

Feedback:
The superficial temporal artery and the inferior maxillary artery are branches of the ECA that communicate with branches of the ophthalmic artery. Flow moves from the ECA to these branches and refills the ophthalmic artery to send flow back into the areas affected by the ipsilateral ICA occlusion.

column1:
Which of the following defines a stroke?

A: nerve conduction abnormalities within the cerebral tissue
B: acute ischemia in cerebral tissue
C: occlusion of the internal carotid artery
D: mobile piece of plaque or thrombus
column2:
Answer: B. acute ischemia in cerebral tissue

Feedback:
Loss of blood flow to an area of cerebral tissue causes cerebral ischemia. All strokes are not caused by ICA occlusion and not every patient with an occluded ICA has a stroke.

column1:
Anemia will _______________ blood flow velocities detected on Doppler evaluation.

A: have no effect on arterial
B: have no effect on venous
C: decrease
D: increase
column2:
Answer: D. increase

Feedback:
Anemia indicates thinner blood with reduced viscosity that would move more quickly in all blood vessels.

column1:
What is an absolute contraindication for sclerotherapy?

A: patient allergy to injectate
B: polycythemia vera
C: vein location above the inguinal ligament
D: vein location immediately over a bony prominence
column2:
Answer: A. patient allergy to injectate

Feedback:
Absolute contraindications are situations which make a particular treatment or procedure absolutely inadvisable. The risk of complications caused by the patient's allergy to the injected substance (sclerosant) far exceed the benefits of the procedure. The patient will be treated using other methods.

column1:
You are preparing for a lower extremity segmental pressure exam on a patient with leg pain. She was admitted to the hospital 2 days ago due to extensive lower extremity DVT. How will you proceed?

A: obtain Doppler waveforms and toe-brachial indices
B: use cuffs only over areas of the leg where DVT was not identified
C: cancel the exam
D: check the chart for anticoagulant medication and if it is listed, proceed normally with the exam
column2:
Answer: A. obtain Doppler waveforms and toe-brachial indices

Feedback:
If a patient has suspected or known acute deep venous thrombosis, use waveform analysis only and/or use toe/brachial indices (TBI). Never place cuffs on a leg with DVT. You could easily cause an embolism with the cuff pressure. Anticoagulant therapy helps to prevent propagation of the clot, it does not provide thrombolytic therapy. The DVT found 2 days ago cannot be recanalized in such a short period of time. A Sonographer should never cancel an exam without referring physician approval.

column1:
What is the Framingham risk score?

A: criteria used to determine appropriate use of vascular ultrasound
B: criteria used to determine appropriate use of physiologic testing
C: gender specific criteria for estimation of 10-year risk of pulmonary embolism
D: gender specific criteria for estimation of 10-year risk of cardiovascular disease
column2:
Answer: D. gender specific criteria for estimation of 10-year risk of cardiovascular disease

Feedback:
Framingham risk score - gender specific criteria for estimation of 10-year risk of cardiovascular disease. Low scores indicate screening exams are unnecessary . High scores may be appropriate for screening in some cases.

column1:
A patient presents for a segmental pressure exam. She states she has had DVT in her right leg three times over the last 2 years. Today she complains of right leg pain and mild swelling, but no color change. She states that the doctor could not find the pedal pulses on the right side. What should you review in the patient chart prior to starting the exam?

A: Surgical history for endoluminal graft placement
B: Medical history for current Coumadin therapy
C: Lab values for hematocrit levels
D: Lab values for lipid levels
column2:
Answer: B. Medical history for current Coumadin therapy

Feedback:
The pressure cuffs can increase the risk for embolization to occur in the right leg. Current Coumadin therapy drastically reduces the ability of the embolism to make it to the lungs if it does form. Hematocrit levels can give you a clue to the blood thickness, but this does not assist you when considering the risk for embolism.

column1:
What console control should be adjusted to improve the quality of the Doppler tracing?

A: increase the PW Doppler scale
B: adjust the placement of the Doppler cursor so it is more parallel to the blood flow
C: raise the baseline
D: reduce the wall filter setting
column2:
Answer: B. adjust the placement of the Doppler cursor so it is more parallel to the blood flow

Feedback:
This image can be improved in multiple ways. The Doppler cursor is nearly perpendicular to the flow in the vein. By moving the Doppler cursor to the portion of the vessel on the left side of the image, the flow would be more parallel to the sound beam. Steering the color box and cursor would also improve the angle of insonation. The highest quality and most accurate Doppler tracing will be produced when the Doppler cursor is parallel to the flow. The PW scale could be reduced to help the waveform fill the Doppler area and improve the display of the low flow velocities. The wall filter could be increased to remove the low level arterial/artifactual Doppler information that appears on the top of the baseline.

column1:
Which of the following describes why there is no aliasing on CW Doppler?

A: CW probes are always lower frequency than PW probes
B: the PW transducer uses a single element and the CW transducer uses two elements
C: CW Doppler does not require beam steering to produce a Doppler waveform
D: CW Doppler does not require a sample volume to produce a Doppler waveform
column2:
Answer: B. the PW transducer uses a single element and the CW transducer uses two elements

Feedback:
CW Doppler uses separate elements for sending and receiving the sound waves. This allows for Doppler evaluation without depth limitations.

column1:
Which of the following describes the correct way to measure the resistive index for a renal artery waveform?

A: place a caliper at the onset of the systolic upstroke and at the early systolic peak
B: place a caliper at the early systolic peak and the end diastolic velocity
C: place a caliper at the onset of the systolic upstroke and at the true systolic peak
D: place a caliper at the true systolic peak and the end diastolic velocity
column2:
Answer: D. place a caliper at the true systolic peak and the end diastolic velocity

Feedback:
Resistive Index - place a caliper at the true systolic peak and the end diastolic velocity; Acceleration Time - place a caliper at the onset of the systolic upstroke and at the early systolic peak

column1:
A thrill is a normal clinical finding with _______________________, but an abnormal clinical finding with___________________________.

A: lower extremity vein graft, lower extremity prosthetic graft
B: thoracic outlet syndrome, Raynaud syndrome
C: recent iliac stent placement, a hemodialysis graft
D: a hemodialysis fistula, recent iliac stent placement
column2:
Answer: D. a hemodialysis fistula, recent iliac stent placement

Feedback:
A thrill is a normal clinical finding with hemodialysis AVF, but an abnormal clinical finding after any invasive procedure of the lower extremity.

column1:
Which of the following is a correct statement describing the guidelines for wearing a mask?

A: Wash your hands, put on gloves, hold the mask over your mouth by touching the front of the mask. tie the upper strings over your ears toward the top of your head and the bottom strings behind your neck
B: Wash your hands, hold the mask over your mouth by touching the front of the mask. tie the upper strings over your ears toward the top of your head and the bottom strings behind your neck
C: Wash your hands, tie the upper strings over your ears toward the top of your head and the bottom strings behind your neck
D: Wash your hands, put on gloves, tie the upper strings over your ears toward the top of your head and the bottom strings behind your neck
column2:
Answer: C. Wash your hands, tie the upper strings over your ears toward the top of your head and the bottom strings behind your neck

Feedback:
Proper procedure for wearing a mask: Wash your hands, tie the upper strings over your ears toward the top of your head and the bottom strings behind your neck. If a gown is required, it goes on next, followed by the gloves at the end. When finished, remove the gloves first, and then untie the bottom strings, followed by the top strings. Do not touch the center part of the mask. Discard the mask properly and wash your hands.

column1:
A patient presents with an order for a lower extremity venous Doppler exam to rule out venous insufficiency. This patient should be evaluated in the _____________ position in order to _______________.

A: supine, increase hydrostatic pressure
B: standing, increase hydrostatic pressure
C: standing, reduce hydrostatic pressure
D: supine, reduce hydrostatic pressure
column2:
Answer: B. standing, increase hydrostatic pressure

Feedback:
The standing position will increase hydrostatic or gravitational pressure, which slows lower extremity venous return. This will fill the veins of the legs with blood making the US and Doppler evaluation much easier. It will also cause superficial varicosities to engorge with blood for better visualization.

column1:
You are performing a renal Doppler exam. What is the best patient position to visualize the full length of the left renal artery?

A: Right lateral decubitus
B: Left lateral decubitus
C: Supine, ipsilateral arm above head
D: Supine, contralateral arm above head
column2:
Answer: A. Right lateral decubitus

Feedback:
A coronal view or an oblique transverse view will best demonstrate the full length of the renal arteries. These images are best obtained when the patient is in the right lateral decubitus position when evaluating the left kidney and artery.

column1:
What is the gold standard method of diagnosing temporal arteritis?

A: biopsy
B: duplex ultrasound
C: CTA
D: MRA
column2:
Answer: A. biopsy

Feedback:
Biopsy of the temporal artery is the gold standard method of diagnosis for temporal arteritis.

column1:
Priapism is an indication for what type of exam?

A: mesenteric duplex
B: carotid duplex
C: penile duplex
D: upper extremity segmental pressure
column2:
Answer: C. penile duplex

Feedback:
Priapism is a painful prolonged erection that occurs without sexual stimuli. It can be caused by veno occlusive disease in the pelvic veins or an AV fistula in the pelvic region

column1:
What will you include in your preliminary report for this patient?

A: There is a possible obstruction of the left subclavian artery.
B: There is a possible obstruction of the right innominate artery or the subclavian artery.
C: There is a possible obstruction of the left innominate artery.
D: There is a possible obstruction of the left innominate artery or the subclavian artery.
column2:
Answer: A. There is a possible obstruction of the left subclavian artery.

Feedback:
Keep in mind, there is no left innominate artery. The left subclavian artery originates from the aortic arch. The pressures indicate a blockage in the left upper arm arteries (subclavian or axillary). The pressure difference between the arms is greater than 20mmHg. The arm with the lower pressure is the arm with the problem.

column1:
Which of the following is a contraindication for US guided compression therapy for a pseudoaneurysm at the groin?

A: Infection at the puncture site
B: A pseudoaneurysm that has been present for more than 72hrs
C: A pseudoaneurysm that measures 3.2cm in diameter
D: Patient that stopped taking Coumadin 3 days ago
column2:
Answer: A. Infection at the puncture site

Feedback:
Contraindications for US guided compression therapy for a pseudoaneurysm: Patient unable to tolerate the procedure due to pain Infection at the puncture site and surrounding tissues Diameter >4cm Stalk diameter >5mm Present for more than 1 month Patients on anticoagulant therapy (must be stopped before compression)

column1:
Which artifact is demonstrated on the image?

A: side lobes
B: reverberation
C: cross talk
D: volume averaging
column2:
Answer: C. cross talk

Feedback:
The Doppler tracing displays a mirror image of the waveform on the opposite side of the baseline. This commonly occurs when sampling vessels adjacent to a highly reflective structure like a bone.

column1:
What is the preferred patient position during catheter retraction in an endovenous laser ablation treatment?

A: seated with legs dangling
B: reverse Trendelenburg
C: Trendelenburg
D: standing
column2:
Answer: C. Trendelenburg

Feedback:
When the catheter is inserted at the start of the procedure, the semi-erect or reverse Trendelenburg position is helpful to better visualize the advancement of the catheter. Once the laser is activated and the catheter is retracted, the patient is placed in the Trendelenburg position to reduce the blood volume in the veins of the leg and facilitate the closure of the vein during the procedure. The laser essentially damages the walls of the veins causing a reduced lumen diameter and thrombus formation. The less blood that's in the vein, the easier it will be to successfully treat the vein.

column1:
Which of the following explains why a patient can receive an US diagnosis of 70% ICA stenosis, but an angiography diagnosis of 50% ICA stenosis?

A: Angiography only evaluates the longitudinal axis of vessel which can lead to over- or under-estimation of the stenosis.
B: Angiography calculates the area stenosis and the US exam offers the diameter stenosis.
C: There was most likely a difference in the patient's cardiac output on the dates the two exams were performed.
D: The patient has severe hyperlipidemia with acute disease progression between the two exams.
column2:
Answer: A. Angiography only evaluates the longitudinal axis of vessel which can lead to over- or under-estimation of the stenosis.

Feedback:
Angiography only evaluates the longitudinal axis of vessel, which can lead to over- or under-estimation of the stenosis. Only the diameter stenosis can be estimated with this method. If the vessel is curved or tortuous, it can make estimation of stenosis difficult on a longitudinal 2-dimensional angio image.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes how to prepare for an intraoperative vascular ultrasound?

A: Cover a low frequency transducer with a sterile sheath that contains gel
B: Use sterile gloves to open the packaging for supplies used in the procedure
C: Cover a high frequency transducer with a sterile sheath that contains gel
D: Gather extra packets of sterile gel to be used on the outside of the sterile sheath
column2:
Answer: C. Cover a high frequency transducer with a sterile sheath that contains gel

Feedback:
Intraoperative ultrasound procedures require a high frequency transducer because the transducer will usually be placed directly on the vessel being evaluated (no other tissue to penetrate). Sterile gel is placed inside the sheath, but not on the vessel or inside the body. Sterile gloves are used to touch any of the sterile instruments, but opening packaging does not require sterile gloves.
